evidence: Top-level packages (api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils) map cleanly onto domain capabilities — a new engineer can immediately explain what each package owns.

api/ is decomposed into SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers — each owning one HTTP domain area; WordSketchApiServer purely wires routes.
config/ cleanly separates GrammarConfig (value object), GrammarConfigLoader (file I/O), GrammarConfigSerializer (JSON output) — three distinct responsibilities with no overlap.
ExplorationService interface in exploration/ provides a stable boundary between the HTTP layer and algorithm classes; api/ depends on the interface, not on SemanticFieldExplorer directly.
All packages have package-info.java explaining purpose; model/package-info.java explicitly documents the two-tier design (pure DTOs vs model objects with derived accessors) and what belongs.

Edge and RelationEdgeType live in model/exploration despite being visualization response primitives (graph edges for D3.js force graphs). The model package-info.java states 'No persistence, I/O, or HTTP concerns belong in this package' — these are presentation/HTTP response concerns, not domain model types.

RadialPlot.java lives in api/ but is purely a visualization data builder (SVG generator with layout constants) — it handles no HttpExchange and has no HTTP concerns. It belongs in a viz/ or api/visualization/ sub-package rather than the flat api/ package.

ExplorationOptions Javadoc states 'Single-seed exploration uses SingleSeedExplorationOptions, which adds the reverseExpansionLimit parameter' but SingleSeedExplorationOptions does not exist anywhere in the codebase. The parameter is passed as a bare int. This is a stale documentation-reality mismatch.

QueryExecutor interface has one production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) and one test stub (StubQueryExecutor). The Javadoc explicitly acknowledges this as a 'testability seam rather than an abstraction boundary' and defers a possible split to 'a second implementation or clearer isolation need' — honest and documented.

ExplorationService interface similarly has one production implementation (SemanticFieldExplorer). Its Javadoc justifies bundling three exploration modes because they 'share the same underlying corpus-query infrastructure and are presented as one coherent feature surface to the HTTP layer' — again explicitly acknowledged.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed (line 434) is a single-line delegation: `return multiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection(seeds, relationConfig, opts)`. SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles is similarly `return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seeds, opts)`. Two of four ExplorationService methods are pure pass-throughs to collaborator objects, creating an extra indirection hop without transformation. The Javadoc calls this a 'coordination facade' but the coordination cost for these two methods is zero.

Handler decomposition (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) is genuine: each class performs real HTTP parameter parsing, domain validation, and response building specific to its endpoint group — not pass-through routing.

HttpApiUtils bundles response-writing and request-parsing in one package-private class. The Javadoc explicitly justifies this co-location ('both sets of methods serve a single concern — handling the HTTP layer') and notes the class is package-private to prevent public API surface pollution. The combined responsibilities are cohesive and every method is non-trivial.

ExploreResponseAssembler is a genuine translation layer: it builds typed Edge lists from ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult model objects, provides two distinct projection methods (collocateResultToExampleMap vs collocateResultToFullMap), and rounds floating-point values. Not a pass-through.

RelationPatternUtils, GrammarConfigSerializer, and GrammarConfigLoader are correctly separated from their associated data carriers (RelationConfig, GrammarConfig) to keep value objects free of IO and JSON-library dependencies — a legitimate abstraction that prevents layering violations.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses a BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String, Object>> builder callback, deduplicating the relation-iteration loop between handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketch — the abstraction is used in exactly two callers and pays for itself in reduced duplication.

Mechanical scan reports 0 delegation-heavy classes, 0 facade modules, 0 typed-dict violations. The 51 indirection hotspots are Java method-chain counts that reflect normal fluent API usage (e.g., `RelationPatternUtils.buildFullPattern(rel, lemma)` chained into executor calls), not deep abstraction stacks.

pom.xml declares only two functional runtime dependencies: blacklab-engine + blacklab-query-parser (both 4.0.0) and jackson-databind + jackson-core (2.17.2); everything else is logging or provided/test scope

Jackson BOM imported via dependencyManagement ensures all Jackson artifacts resolve to the same version; jackson-core is also pinned explicitly with a comment explaining that a transitive dep would otherwise resolve an older version — a deliberate, well-documented version-conflict fix
log4j-to-slf4j bridge routes BlackLab's internal log4j calls through SLF4J/Logback, preventing dual logging-framework conflicts on the classpath
JSpecify declared as 'provided' scope — correct choice; annotation-only dep not bundled into shaded JAR
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose observed: single JSON library (Jackson), single logging facade (SLF4J), single HTTP abstraction (JDK built-in httpserver)

blacklab-query-parser is legitimately used in two production files: BlackLabQueryExecutor.java uses ContextualQueryLanguageParser and CollocateQueryHelper.java uses CorpusQueryLanguageParser — explicit declaration justified
No circular dependency cycles visible; package structure is clean with clear layering (api → exploration/query → model)
All test dependencies (junit-jupiter-api, junit-jupiter-engine, junit-platform-launcher) are correctly scoped to 'test'

Phase-decomposed helpers in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) and MultiSeedExplorer (fetchCollocatesPerSeed, identifyCommonCollocates, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates, aggregateCollocateStats) use consistent private-method extraction style across related classes

Guard clauses applied uniformly: null/empty lemma guards in BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeCollocations and executeDependencyPattern, sampleSize==0 early return in CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits, seed blank check in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern

Java records used appropriately for internal data holders throughout (CollocateSearch, HitRecord, AggregatedCollocateStats, SeedCollocateData, ExecutedSketch, BcqlRequest, CommonExploreParams) — keeps local state explicit and bounded

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples calls RelationPatternUtils.buildFullPattern(rel.get(), req.seed(), req.collocate()) twice in the non-fallback branch (lines 61 and 62) to execute the query and then to capture the pattern string — a redundant computation that should be hoisted to a local variable

Main.parseIntParam and Main.parseDoubleParam are structurally identical (null-check, parse, System.exit(1) on failure, throw AssertionError unreachable) differing only in parse method and error message noun — minor structural duplication within a single class

ExploreResponseAssembler is a dedicated, stateless translator at the API seam: it owns all model-to-presentation conversion for ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult, keeping handler classes free of mapping concerns.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader/GrammarConfigSerializer are cleanly separated into data holder, IO+parsing, and serialization — each carries only the concern its name implies.

HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a consistent, centralized mapping from IllegalArgumentException/IOException to HTTP 400/500 across all handlers, so error propagation across the HTTP boundary is predictable.

ExplorationHandlers extracts shared parsing into parseCommonExploreParams, resolveRelationConfig, and parseSeedSet, preventing duplication across three related handlers while keeping per-handler variation inline.
Handler inputs are typed as records (ConcordanceExamplesRequest, BcqlRequest, CommonExploreParams) so the HTTP-to-domain translation step is explicit and self-documenting.

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples calls RelationPatternUtils.buildFullPattern twice (lines 61-62): once as the query argument passed to executor.executeBcqlQuery and once to assign bcqlQuery for the response. The result is computed, discarded, and recomputed with identical arguments — a seam where a temporary variable should have been used.

buildExploreResponseEnvelope accepts two generic Map<String,Object> parameters — paramExtras and variantFields — that are structurally distinct (paramExtras is merged into the nested 'parameters' sub-map; variantFields is merged at the top level), but the method signature gives no indication of this difference. Callers must know which map ends up where through documentation rather than type.

ComparisonResult is a mutable-style class exposing fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), specific(), and specificTo() all as re-streaming methods over the same backing list, alongside the single-pass summaryCounts(). The parallel presence of both access paths at the seam encourages callers to stream the list multiple times without obvious reason to prefer summaryCounts().

GrammarConfigLoader and GrammarConfigSerializer each instantiate a private static ObjectMapper, while HttpApiUtils has a third. Three separate ObjectMapper instances cross the config/API layer boundary; not a correctness defect but a mild seam inconsistency.

SketchHandlers.handleSurfaceRelations and handleDepRelations are single-line pass-through wrappers: each does nothing but forward (exchange, RelationType.X) to handleRelationsForType — no auth, logging, or transformation added

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() duplicates the exact try/catch/IllegalStateException pattern from createDefaultEnglish(String configPath) instead of delegating to it; zero-arg overload could be a one-liner calling the string overload

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial logs the full request body at debug level: logger.debug("Radial: body = {}", body) — body can be several kilobytes of JSON; this is a nosy object-dump pattern, even if benign at debug level

GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader Javadoc includes a trivial usage example (try (Reader r = new StringReader(jsonContent)) { GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader(r); }) that restates the method signature without adding information — the method purpose is self-evident from the signature
GrammarConfigLoader.java:127-135 has a `validateNoLegacyKeys` guard that explicitly rejects the old 'copulas' key with a helpful migration message, showing completed cleanup of one old API pattern.

GrammarConfigLoader.java:164: comment '// Canonical field name is "pattern"; cql_pattern is no longer supported' documents a completed field rename, but unlike 'copulas' there is no guard that catches someone accidentally using 'cql_pattern' — they receive the opaque error 'has no pattern field' instead of a helpful redirect.

WordSketchApiServer.java:53: '// TODO: migrate to Undertow or Jetty if a multi-module build or custom classloader is needed' — the TODO is explicitly gated on a future need that has not arisen; the adjacent comment explains the current choice is intentional ('Used intentionally for zero external runtime deps').

GrammarConfigLoader.java exposes two `createDefaultEnglish` overloads: the zero-arg variant uses a hidden system-property lookup (`-Dgrammar.config`) and the Javadoc for the String-arg overload says it 'avoids the hidden system-property dependency of createDefaultEnglish()'. Both remain public; Main.java still calls the zero-arg version — not a migration blockage but a mild API surface tension.
No @deprecated annotations exist anywhere in production source; no mixed JS/TS files; no moment/dayjs, axios/fetch, or class/functional component coexistence patterns.

api/ has 11 files: 10 are HTTP-layer classes (handlers, server, utils, assembler, exception) — cohesive. RadialPlot.java is the outlier: a pure SVG visualization computation class with no HttpExchange/HttpApiUtils dependency.

RadialPlot test lives in src/test/java/.../viz/RadialPlotTest.java (package pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.viz), while the source lives in api/. The test author placed it in a dedicated viz package, signalling recognition that it is not an API class.

VisualizationHandlers.java imports pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api.RadialPlot — a within-package import that only exists because RadialPlot is co-located, not because it belongs there architecturally.

All other packages are purpose-driven and well-scoped: config/ (grammar loading+serialization), exploration/ (service logic), model/exploration/ (DTOs), query/ (execution), indexer/blacklab/ (indexing), utils/ (pure utilities).
model/package-info.java documents the intentional design split between model/ root and model/exploration/ subpackage, showing deliberate organizational thinking.
No flat directory overload: api/ is the largest at 11 files but 10 of them are clearly HTTP-layer (handlers, server, utils). model/exploration/ also has 11 files — all exploration DTOs, cohesive.
No root-level stragglers: all source files are under named packages; Main.java at the root of word_sketch is the genuine high-fan-in entry point.

QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern Javadoc states 'The headword lemma must be embedded in bcqlPattern' as a caller contract, but BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern silently returns Collections.emptyList() (line 229-233) when CqlUtils.extractHeadword returns null/empty, instead of throwing IllegalArgumentException. This creates a precondition-vs-silent-empty mismatch.

RelationPatternUtils.buildFullPattern(RelationConfig, @Nullable String headword, String collocateLemma): the second String parameter collocateLemma is not annotated @Nullable but the implementation at line 37 defensively handles null/blank with 'if (collocateLemma == null || collocateLemma.isBlank()) return withHead'. Contract annotation and implementation diverge.

executeCqlQuery in BlackLabQueryExecutor (lines 109-140) creates SnippetResult with raw XML concatenation (parts[0]+parts[1]+parts[2]) stored in the 'sentence' field. The ConcordanceResult.sentence() accessor contract is ambiguous — callers expecting plain text would be surprised. The method is declared in the public QueryExecutor interface but is never called from any handler, limiting practical impact.

QueryExecutor interface documentation is unusually thorough: each method distinguishes BCQL vs CQL parsers, fallback semantics, @Nullable annotations, and logDice behaviour. Exception contracts (IOException wrapping, IllegalArgumentException -> 400) are consistent across all 5 method signatures.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() clearly documents the two-tier exception wrapping pattern (checked IOException wrapped into unchecked IllegalStateException), including the rationale. Both overloads share the same documented contract.
HttpApiUtils methods uniformly document their throws contracts and cross-reference wrapWithErrorHandling, creating a coherent error-handling contract chain across the API layer.

SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchSeedCollocates and MultiSeedExplorer.fetchCollocatesPerSeed document in-code why the fallback is intentionally absent in multi-seed mode — this is explicit contract reasoning at the call-site level.
RelationPatternUtils.buildCollocateReversePattern documents @throws IllegalStateException for both absent relationType and unknown POS group, and the implementation faithfully throws in both cases.

BlackLabSnippetParser.detokenize() returns null only when its argument is null. In trimToSentence(), `assembled` is built from string concatenation (never null), so `detokenize(assembled)` never returns null; the fallback branch `detokenized != null ? detokenized : assembled` at line 88 is dead code.

Same pattern at line 130 in extractPlainTextFromXml(): `text` is the result of chained .replaceAll().replaceAll().trim() on a guarded non-null string, so the fallback `detokenized != null ? detokenized : text` is never taken.

Main.java uses `System.exit(1); throw new AssertionError("unreachable");` in parseIntParam and parseDoubleParam — the AssertionError throw is dead code, but it is a documented compiler-satisfaction idiom and labelled 'unreachable', so it is intentional.
HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams catches UnsupportedEncodingException with a comment 'UTF-8 is always supported; this branch is unreachable in practice' — intentional dead code with explanatory comment.

Guard clauses throughout the codebase (null/empty checks before processing) are used consistently and correctly in BlackLabQueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, and the exploration layer.
No identical if/else branches found. Boolean logic is direct throughout; no `if x: return true else: return false` anti-patterns observed.
Control flow in BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeCqlQuery, executeDependencyPattern, and executeCollocations is straightforward with clean early-return paths when lemma or deprel is absent.
VisualizationHandlers switch over RenderMode uses null-case and SIGNED explicitly, which is exhaustive and correct for the current single-enum-value definition.

JSpecify @Nullable/@NonNull annotations are used consistently on public API surfaces: QueryExecutor interface, HttpApiUtils, ExplorationHandlers, MultiSeedExplorer, ExploreResponseAssembler, SemanticFieldExplorer — forming a solid annotated core.

BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLastLemma() returns String but Javadoc says 'or null' and the implementation does return null; no @Nullable annotation present (line 211). All sibling methods in the same class that return null are correctly annotated @Nullable.

BlackLabSnippetParser.extractPosFromMatch(String matchText) accepts null at runtime (body has 'if (matchText == null || matchText.isEmpty()) return null') but the parameter is typed String with no @Nullable.

RelationPatternUtils.computeCollocatePosGroup(String pattern): parameter accepts null (body: 'if (pattern == null) return PosGroup.OTHER') but lacks @Nullable. Private helpers extractLabelContent and resolvePosGroupFromPrefix return null without @Nullable on their return types.

CollocateQueryHelper protected no-arg constructor explicitly sets 'this.index = null' while the field is typed BlackLabIndex with no @Nullable annotation. The class Javadoc explains this is a test seam, but the field type contradicts the runtime-null state.
Main.getParam(Map<String,String>, String...) Javadoc: 'Returns the first non-null value for any of the given keys, or null.' Return type is bare String without @Nullable.
VisualizationHandlers.RenderMode.parse(String value) returns RenderMode but can return null when value is null/blank; no @Nullable annotation on the return type.
Class names are precise domain nouns: ConcordanceHandlers, CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, ExploreResponseAssembler — each communicates responsibility without ambiguity
Boolean predicate methods are honest: isFullyShared(), isPartiallyShared(), isSpecific() on CollocateProfile genuinely return booleans matching their names
QueryExecutor method set uses distinct discriminative verbs: executeCollocations, executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern — callers cannot confuse them
handleDepSketch vs handleDependencyRelationQuery in SketchHandlers.java — 'Dep' abbreviated in one method name, spelled out as 'Dependency' in the sibling method in the same class

SENT_BOUND_LEFT, SENT_BOUND_RIGHT, XML_SENT_OPEN, XML_SENT_CLOSE in BlackLabSnippetParser use the 'SENT' abbreviation while the surrounding codebase consistently spells out 'sentence' (STATE_SENTENCES, sentencesPerChunk, ConversionStats.sentences)
collocMap (line 66 MultiSeedExplorer) abbreviates 'collocate' in a codebase that otherwise spells it out everywhere: collocateSharedCount, collocatePattern, perSeedCollocates, collocatePosition
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling() provides a uniform top-level boundary for all HTTP handlers: IAE→400, RequestEntityTooLargeException→413, IOException→500, Exception→500.
CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery() and performCollocateSearch() wrap InvalidQuery (a user-input validation failure) as IOException, which wrapWithErrorHandling maps to HTTP 500 instead of 400.

CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery() contains catch (InvalidQuery | IllegalArgumentException e) → sendError 400, but executor.executeBcqlQuery() always wraps InvalidQuery into IOException before it returns, making the InvalidQuery branch of that catch dead code.
GrammarConfigLoader uses a documented two-tier contract: throws IOException for I/O, throws IllegalStateException for startup-context callers — clearly intentional and consistent.
BlackLabSnippetParser null-returning methods are annotated @Nullable throughout; null semantics are explicit and documented.
RuntimeException in CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency() is wrapped as IOException to keep the executor interface uniform — intentional but obscures the underlying cause type.
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries() catches IOException per-relation and adds to a relationErrors list rather than aborting — intentional partial-failure pattern, explicitly documented.
Dependency graph is a clean DAG: model ← utils/config/query ← exploration ← api. No runtime import cycles detected.

ExplorationService (exploration package) is a proper interface boundary between api and the exploration implementation, correctly preventing api from depending on SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, or CollocateProfileComparator directly.
model package imports nothing from the project; indexer package imports nothing from the project — both are fully isolated leaf/root packages.

SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, and CorpusQueryHandlers in the api package hold a direct dependency on QueryExecutor (query package) and contain significant non-HTTP business logic: buildSketch(), executeRelationQueries(), formatWordSketchResult(), buildSurfaceRelationEntry() etc. — business logic that the parallel ExplorationService boundary shields for the exploration subsystem.
RadialPlot (SVG renderer, 242 lines, no HTTP dependencies) is physically located in the api package, blurring the api boundary with a presentational utility that has no HTTP concerns.

RelationConfig.java line 18 Javadoc contains {@link pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api.SketchHandlers} — an upward documentation reference from the config layer to the api layer, signalling that the config author reasoned about its callers rather than just its own role.
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty("grammar.config", ...) at call time, not at class-load time — fragile-in-testing pattern is explicitly avoided.
HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader() reads System.getProperty("cors.allow.origin", ...) at per-request time, not at field initialisation — again correctly deferred.
No System.getenv() calls anywhere in the codebase — no environment-variable reads at class load time.

All subsystem dependencies in Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand are wired via explicit constructor injection in a clear, documented order: BlackLabQueryExecutor → GrammarConfig → CollocateProfileComparator → MultiSeedExplorer → SemanticFieldExplorer → WordSketchApiServer.
Shutdown hook uses AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer> registered before server construction so the hook always has a consistent reference even if construction throws partway through.

Three separate static final ObjectMapper instances exist across the codebase: HttpApiUtils.MAPPER (package-private), GrammarConfigLoader.MAPPER (private), GrammarConfigSerializer.MAPPER (private). All are initialised at class-load time. ObjectMapper() construction is pure Java allocation (no I/O, no network), so the side-effect is benign, but the three instances could silently diverge if Jackson module registration or feature flags are ever needed.
HttpApiUtils.MAPPER is package-private (no private modifier), making it accessible to all classes in the api package and creating an implicit shared-singleton dependency within that package.
No circular initialisation dependencies found; no lazy/conditional imports masking circular state.

All five handler classes (ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) follow an identical structural pattern: package-private class, no public interface, single-field-per-dependency constructor, SLF4J logger, void handle* methods — perfectly consistent across the api package

All static-method utility classes (RelationPatternUtils, RelationUtils, CqlUtils, LogDiceUtils, MathUtils, GrammarConfigSerializer, ExploreResponseAssembler) follow the same idiom: public final class, private no-arg constructor, pure static methods — no drift between config, utils, and api packages

All four enum types with JSON labels (RelationEdgeType, SharingCategory, PosGroup, RelationType) consistently use a label field, a label() accessor, and a toString() override — same pattern repeated without exception
Every package in the tree has a package-info.java file: api, config, exploration, indexer/blacklab, model, model/exploration, query, utils — no package is undocumented

RadialPlot.java sits in the api package alongside HTTP handlers; the api package-info says 'HTTP request handlers, routing utilities, and response assembly' — RadialPlot is a pure SVG rendering class with no HTTP concerns, formerly in a viz/ package (per CLAUDE.md). No rationale documents why it was moved into api rather than a dedicated rendering package

ComparisonResult uses a public constructor (new ComparisonResult(nouns, collocates)) while sibling ExplorationResult uses a private-field approach with no private constructor annotation and a static factory empty(). The model/package-info explains why both are classes rather than records, but does not address the construction-pattern asymmetry between them

ExplorationOptions Javadoc line 8 reads 'Single-seed exploration uses SingleSeedExplorationOptions, which adds the reverseExpansionLimit parameter specific to the reverse-lookup phase' — no such class exists in the codebase; exploreByPattern takes reverseExpansionLimit as a plain int parameter. This is stale documentation left over from a planned but unimplemented extraction
274 @Test methods across 36 test files covering api, exploration, query, config, indexer, utils, and viz packages
StubQueryExecutor (shared base stub), TestExchangeFactory.MockExchange, and GrammarConfigHelper are dedicated test-infrastructure classes used consistently across tests — no ad-hoc duplication
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest and BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest both carry @Disabled on their live-index nested class/test; the real Lucene query path is never exercised in CI

BlackLabIntegrationTest is a CI-compatible integration test that exercises XML parsing → collocate extraction → logDice ranking using a StubCollocateHelper subclass — a well-designed substitute, but it bypasses BlackLabQueryExecutor entirely

WordSketchApiServerTest starts a real HTTP server on port 18080 and verifies route routing, but always uses the empty StubQueryExecutor, so no non-trivial data flow through the full pipeline is ever verified
ExplorationHandlersTest and SketchHandlersTest cover both 200 happy-path and 400 error-path for all major endpoints
SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest.RecordingExecutor captures CQL pattern strings and asserts that they contain 'NN' — minor coupling to internal pattern construction details
No Mockito or mock-framework usage anywhere; hand-rolled stubs are decoupled from internal implementation
No snapshot tests, no timing-dependent assertions, no test-ordering annotations — no fragility patterns

CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery catches `InvalidQuery | IllegalArgumentException` inline at line 50 and calls HttpApiUtils.sendError(400) directly, bypassing the wrapWithErrorHandling contract used by every other handler. This means RuntimeException and unhandled IOException paths in that handler silently escape to the JDK HTTP server's unchecked handler rather than returning a JSON error body.

ExplorationService.exploreByPattern takes 4 positional params: (String seed, RelationConfig, ExplorationOptions, int reverseExpansionLimit), while exploreMultiSeed takes 3: (Set<String> seeds, RelationConfig, ExplorationOptions). The extra positional int breaks the symmetry of the service interface — reverseExpansionLimit is semantically an option and could live in ExplorationOptions alongside topCollocates/minLogDice/minShared.

ExplorationOptions.java Javadoc (line 8) explicitly references a SingleSeedExplorationOptions type that does not exist in the codebase. The actual code passes reverseExpansionLimit as a positional parameter, so the documented design intent and the implemented API are misaligned.

All five handler constructors accept their dependencies in consistent order (service/executor first, GrammarConfig second) and all public handler methods follow the same `void handle*(HttpExchange) throws IOException` signature — uniform pattern across SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, and VisualizationHandlers.

HttpApiUtils.requireParam, parseIntParam, and parseDoubleParam provide consistent parameter extraction with a shared validation and error-throwing contract; all query-string parsing across all handlers flows through these helpers.

buildExploreResponseEnvelope in ExplorationHandlers centralises response structure for the three exploration endpoints (explore, explore-multi, compare), giving them consistent status/parameters/seeds envelope shapes.

QueryExecutor.executeCollocations embeds the lemma as a separate first parameter while executeSurfacePattern embeds the lemma inside the bcqlPattern string itself — the two collocate-lookup methods follow different calling conventions, requiring callers to understand which convention applies to each.

WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: 'the server binds exclusively to the loopback address (127.0.0.1) so it is never exposed on any external network interface. All endpoints are unauthenticated — access control is provided by the host firewall and the loopback-only bind.'
HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader() is called in every response-sending method (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse), ensuring consistent CORS policy across all endpoints.
The cors.allow.origin system property can be overridden to '*' at runtime — a warning is logged but the widened trust boundary is applied uniformly to all endpoints with no per-endpoint guard.

Input validation (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam, MAX_PARAM_LENGTH) is applied consistently across all handler classes: ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers.

The /api/bcql POST endpoint accepts arbitrary BCQL corpus queries (up to MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH=1024 chars and MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH=20 token constraints) — this is the most privileged endpoint but receives the same trust level (none) as read-only sketch endpoints. No rate limiting or additional scrutiny is applied.
No auth middleware, decorators, permission constants, or role checks exist anywhere in the codebase — the design is uniformly open within the loopback trust boundary.

Layer separation is explicit and consistent: api/ owns HTTP concerns, exploration/ owns algorithms, query/ owns corpus access, config/ owns grammar data, model/ owns DTOs — no layer bleeds into another.
ExploreResponseAssembler is a focused translation class with no state: all model-to-JSON conversion lives here, keeping ExplorationHandlers and ExplorationResult free of presentation concerns.
RelationPatternUtils is extracted from RelationConfig so the record stays a plain data carrier — a textbook separation of data and computation.

SemanticFieldExplorer has a documented dual role: it owns the single-seed algorithm directly AND acts as a coordination facade for MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator. This makes it larger than the consistently-scoped algorithm classes beside it.

buildExploreResponseEnvelope in ExplorationHandlers takes two Map<String,Object> parameters (paramExtras, variantFields) that differ only in where their entries land (inside 'parameters' sub-map vs top-level). The distinction is non-obvious from the signature and the Javadoc comment is required to understand the contract.

handleSemanticFieldExamples builds its own response map with 8 manual put() calls rather than delegating to buildExploreResponseEnvelope, while the other three handlers in the same class use the shared envelope builder — an asymmetry with no documented rationale.

GrammarConfigLoader exposes four loading methods with two different exception contracts: load(Path) and fromReader(Reader) throw checked IOException; createDefaultEnglish(String) and createDefaultEnglish() wrap IOException in RuntimeException. Callers must check which variant they hold to know what to catch.
CollocateQueryHelper is package-private and cleanly scoped to the query package, with a no-arg protected constructor as an explicit testing seam — good design hygiene.

SketchHandlers.routeSketchRequest dispatches on path parts by splitting a string and checking array indices. This is brittle if the URL structure changes, but it is contained in one method and the routing table is small.

ExplorationService interface bundles four operations (single-seed explore, multi-seed explore, compare, fetch examples) with a shared Javadoc rationale explaining why they are co-located. The grouping is deliberate and explained.

strengths: Top-level package decomposition is domain-aligned: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils each own a coherent capability with no namespace confusion.
HTTP handler splitting in api/ (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers) gives each domain area predictable ownership; WordSketchApiServer is a thin router.

config/ cleanly separates loading (GrammarConfigLoader), data (GrammarConfig/RelationConfig), serialization (GrammarConfigSerializer) and utility (RelationPatternUtils, RelationUtils) by responsibility.

ExplorationService interface provides a stable public boundary between the HTTP layer and algorithm internals, insulating api/ from changes to SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator.
Package-info.java docs exist on all packages and explain ownership rationale; model/package-info.java explicitly documents design decisions (why classes instead of records, sub-package structure).
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are contained boundary leaks rather than systemic ownership confusion: visualization-specific types (Edge, RelationEdgeType, RadialPlot) have been placed in domain model and API packages without a coherent 'where do visualization concerns live?' answer, and one stale Javadoc reference creates a documentation mismatch. These do not undermine the overall decomposition but do create predictable confusion at the model/presentation boundary.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The overall package architecture is clean and domain-aligned — a new engineer can explain why each top-level package exists and its stated responsibility is trustworthy. What pulls the score below 80 is that visualization response types (Edge, RelationEdgeType) are housed in the domain model package despite the model package-info.java explicitly excluding presentation concerns, and RadialPlot (an SVG builder) sits in the flat api/ package without a dedicated visualization home. Together these create a 'where do I put visualization types?' inconsistency that a new engineer would struggle to resolve from the existing structure alone. The stale SingleSeedExplorationOptions Javadoc reference is a minor additional documentation-reality gap. Placing this at 74 reflects solid ownership with a recurring but contained boundary blur — above 70 because the decomposition is genuinely useful and trustworthy at the subsystem level, below 80 because the model/presentation boundary has visible leaks.

strengths: Every production interface (QueryExecutor, ExplorationService) carries explicit Javadoc rationale for the single-implementation design, distinguishing testability seams from architectural boundaries — a level of self-awareness that prevents accidental over-engineering.

Handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, etc.) add genuine value at each layer: HTTP parameter parsing, domain validation, fallback logic, and response shaping — they are not thin routing wrappers.

ExploreResponseAssembler cleanly isolates model-to-JSON translation so that ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult stay free of presentation concerns; two distinct projection methods (example-only vs full-scored) reflect real caller differences.
RelationPatternUtils and GrammarConfigSerializer are correctly extracted from their data carriers, preventing JSON-library and pattern-building concerns from leaking into value objects.
The model package (Edge, ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, etc.) contains zero HTTP, IO, or persistence concerns — model integrity is maintained across the entire codebase.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The one notable structural pattern is a coordination facade (SemanticFieldExplorer) that directly owns only one of the four ExplorationService methods while passing the other two through to collaborator objects unchanged — a mild redundancy that adds an indirection hop without providing translation, policy, or isolation benefit.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase sits comfortably above the 'significant drag' threshold (60): abstractions are documented, earn their place, and the model layer is clean. The main pull-down is the ExplorationService/SemanticFieldExplorer arrangement where two of four interface methods are pure one-line delegations to inner collaborators, and the two single-implementation interfaces (though justified) represent overhead that a future developer must understand before concluding the indirection is worthwhile. This pushes the score below the 'solid but uneven' band (80), landing at 74 — the codebase is thoughtfully designed with honest documentation of trade-offs, but stops short of exemplary because the coordination facade adds modest redundancy that the current scale does not require.

strengths: Dependency set is exceptionally lean: two functional runtime artifacts (BlackLab and Jackson), a logging stack, and a provided annotation library — no unused or speculative inclusions
Jackson BOM import paired with explicit jackson-core pin demonstrates deliberate version management, actively preventing transitive version downgrade conflicts
log4j-to-slf4j bridge cleanly handles the impedance between BlackLab's logging and the project's SLF4J/Logback stack, avoiding dual-logging-framework classpath issues
JSpecify is correctly placed in 'provided' scope so nullability annotations stay out of the shaded fat-JAR
No duplicated libraries for any purpose (no moment+dayjs, no lodash-for-one-function pattern): each dependency slot is occupied by exactly one well-chosen library
dependency_health.issue_character: No dependency health defects were identified; the minor operational friction around BlackLab's non-Maven-Central distribution is documented and mitigated.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency graph is minimal, purposeful, and conflict-free. Version alignment is handled via BOM plus one explicit override with an explanatory comment — exactly the pattern a well-maintained Maven project should use. The only knock against a perfect score is that BlackLab 4.0.0 is not published to Maven Central and requires a local install script as a prerequisite, introducing a fragile onboarding step that could silently break CI pipelines not following the documented pre-step; this is operational friction rather than a code-level defect. Overall this sits comfortably in the 85–90 'solid, no meaningful issues' band.

strengths: Phase-decomposition style is systematic and consistent across all exploration classes — each multi-step algorithm is split into named private helpers at the same granularity, making the calling flow read as a readable recipe
Guard clauses are applied early and uniformly across all query-dispatch methods, keeping happy-path logic unindented and intention-clear
Internal data carriers use Java records throughout (CollocateSearch, HitRecord, SeedCollocateData, AggregatedCollocateStats, etc.), keeping state mutation explicit, local, and non-shared

CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits and scoreHits separate I/O-heavy hit iteration from pure scoring in two clearly-named phases with matching Javadoc rationale — proportionate extraction that pays for itself
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are isolated redundancies (a double method call, near-identical CLI helpers) rather than systemic local complexity — they represent small missed simplifications rather than a recurring design problem.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase performs strongly at the local level: function internals are concise, extraction is balanced, guard patterns are systematic, and internal data-carrier records are used consistently and appropriately. The two identified issues (double buildFullPattern call in ConcordanceHandlers and duplicate parseIntParam/parseDoubleParam structure in Main) are both small and local, without cascading impact. Against the global anchor of 80 ('developer regularly encounters local complexity that makes functions harder to follow'), this codebase sits above that threshold — friction is infrequent and non-pervasive. The score lands at 83: solid but not exemplary, held back by a handful of missed simplifications rather than structural problems.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler cleanly centralises all model-to-presentation translation at the API seam, so ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult carry no HTTP or JSON concerns.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader/GrammarConfigSerializer follow a clean three-way separation: data, IO+parsing, serialization — boundaries are explicit and each class carries exactly one concern.

wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single, consistent error-propagation seam for all HTTP handlers, making the mapping from domain exceptions to HTTP status codes predictable across the whole API layer.
Handler request parameters are packaged as typed records (ConcordanceExamplesRequest, BcqlRequest, CommonExploreParams), making the HTTP-to-domain handoff explicit rather than a raw Map pass-through.
ExplorationHandlers shares parsing utilities (parseCommonExploreParams, resolveRelationConfig, parseSeedSet) across three related handlers without over-generalising into a tangled base class.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are isolated seam-level infelicities — a redundant double-call at one boundary, an untyped two-map method signature whose structural contract is conveyed only by documentation, and a parallel re-streaming API on a result object — rather than systemic glue-code entropy or tangled cross-cutting back-and-forth between layers.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits comfortably above the 60 'significant drag' threshold: the integration seams are well-structured, translation is centralised, error propagation is consistent, and the handler decomposition is clean. What holds it below 80 ('solid but uneven') is a small set of recurring signature/contract honesty issues at the handoff points — the buildExploreResponseEnvelope map-pair convention, the double-call in ConcordanceHandlers, and ComparisonResult's redundant re-streaming surface — that collectively make certain seams less self-explanatory than the rest. The overall pattern is strong with a handful of isolated rough edges, landing at 73.

strengths: Javadoc comments throughout explain design decisions and WHY, not just WHAT — e.g. HttpApiUtils class-level Javadoc explains why response and parameter concerns coexist in one class rather than restating what the methods do
Method names are consistently domain-specific: executeDependencyPattern, exploreByPattern, resolveRelationAlias, deriveTokenPosition — no generic handleData or processItem placeholders in domain code
Error handling is centralised in wrapWithErrorHandling rather than copy-pasted into every route handler — the codebase resists the boilerplate-duplication pattern
No entry/exit logging on methods; logging is present only where state changes or warnings are meaningful (e.g. skipping a malformed relation, CORS wildcard warning)
SemanticFieldExplorer class Javadoc provides a worked algorithm example with concrete output — this is domain explanation, not code restatement
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: Issues are isolated and structural rather than pervasive: a pair of trivially thin pass-through wrappers, one case of duplicated exception handling that could delegate, and one nosy debug log dumping a full request body — the pattern is 'AI-generated convenience skeletons left unfactored' rather than systemic commentary bloat or defensive overengineering.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase mostly avoids the canonical AI-debt hallmarks: comments explain reasoning rather than restating code, names are domain-anchored, and there is no defensive null-checking of typed values or copy-pasted error handling across the whole codebase. The deductions come from three localised issues: two literal one-line pass-through wrappers (handleSurfaceRelations/handleDepRelations), one case of duplicated try/catch boilerplate between the two createDefaultEnglish overloads that should have been a delegation, and one full-body debug dump. These are real but narrow problems confined to a single handler class and one loader method. The overall quality sits solidly in the 'solid but uneven' band — strong in most places, with a handful of unfactored conveniences that match typical LLM-generated skeleton patterns.

strengths: The 'copulas' key migration is fully complete and guarded: `validateNoLegacyKeys` rejects configs containing the old key with a clear, actionable message pointing to the new pattern.
No deprecated symbols are actively called: there are no @deprecated annotations anywhere in production code and no evidence of old APIs being invoked by active paths.
The codebase is uniformly pure Java — no mixed JS/TS files, no competing HTTP-client or date-library generations, no class vs functional component split.
The 'cql_pattern → pattern' field rename is complete in all production config and code; the comment on GrammarConfigLoader:164 documents the completed rename cleanly.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The single substantive issue is a minor asymmetry in legacy-key guarding: the 'copulas' migration has a catch-and-redirect guard while the 'cql_pattern' rename does not, producing a confusing error for any operator who migrates a config file from an old format. Everything else is benign: a conditional TODO that is explicitly gated on a future need and two coexisting overloads of `createDefaultEnglish` where both remain valid but one is now preferred.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: This codebase scores well on the incomplete_migration dimension because there are no genuinely coexisting old-and-new API surfaces, no deprecated-but-called symbols, and no stale migration shims. The main deduction is the missing guard for `cql_pattern` in GrammarConfigLoader — the migration away from that field name is complete, but a user arriving with an old config file will get a confusing 'no pattern field' error rather than 'cql_pattern is no longer supported'. That is a minor diagnostic gap, not an incomplete migration. The conditional TODO in WordSketchApiServer and the two `createDefaultEnglish` overloads are design notes rather than migration debt. Placing the score at 88 rather than 90+ reflects the single missing guard and the mild `createDefaultEnglish` API surface tension.

strengths: Purpose-driven package names with clear ownership: config/, query/, exploration/, indexer/blacklab/, utils/ each contain exactly the files they advertise and nothing else.

Clean logic/data split: exploration service logic lives in exploration/, while all exploration DTOs live in model/exploration/ — a deliberate two-layer separation documented in model/package-info.java.

model/package-info.java explicitly documents the intentional design decisions (why QueryResults stays at model root, why exploration types get a subpackage), giving future developers navigable rationale.

config/ cohesively groups all grammar config concerns (GrammarConfig value object, GrammarConfigLoader IO machinery, GrammarConfigSerializer for API responses, RelationConfig, RelationType, RelationUtils, RelationPatternUtils) with no cross-concern leakage.
No root-level file stragglers: Main.java is the genuine high-fan-in entry point and the only class at the word_sketch root level.
package_organization.issue_character: A single isolated misplacement: RadialPlot.java — a pure SVG computation class — lives in the HTTP handler package (api/) when its own test suite already treats it as a distinct viz-layer concern.
package_organization.score_rationale: The package structure shows clear, deliberate organization: purpose-driven names, documented design decisions, and a clean logic/data split between exploration/ and model/exploration/. The only structural defect is RadialPlot.java residing in api/ despite having no HTTP dependencies and being tested from a separate viz test package — a visible package/test zone discrepancy. At the 80 anchor ('solid but uneven'), this codebase sits at or just above that level: the organization is genuinely strong, and the single misplacement is isolated and low-impact, but it is a real boundary violation that the test infrastructure already acknowledges.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface Javadoc is exceptionally detailed: each method documents its parser variant (CQL vs BCQL), fallback strategy, @Nullable/@NonNull parameter semantics, and IOException wrapping contract, giving implementors and callers a precise behavioral specification.

The wrapWithErrorHandling / IAE-to-400 error contract chain is documented consistently across HttpApiUtils, all handler classes, and the parseBcqlRequest method, so the expected exception-to-HTTP-status mapping is unambiguous.

GrammarConfigLoader documents the two-tier exception contract (checked IOException -> unchecked IllegalStateException) with rationale in both createDefaultEnglish() overloads, enabling callers to choose the appropriate variant for their context.
RelationPatternUtils.buildCollocateReversePattern has a precise @throws contract that matches the implementation exactly, with no unhandled switch arms silently returning wrong values.

SemanticFieldExplorer explicitly documents the intentional absence of a fallback in MultiSeedExplorer.fetchCollocatesPerSeed with a cross-reference, turning a potential confusion into a documented design decision.
contract_coherence.issue_character: The contract issues are isolated rather than systemic: two specific method-level divergences (a precondition documented as caller responsibility but silently swallowed in the implementation, and a missing @Nullable annotation) plus one unused interface method whose implementation would produce surprising output if called.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase operates at a high level of contract coherence — interfaces are richly documented, exception contracts are consistent, and side-effect-free vs. exception-throwing paths are clearly separated. The main drag is a single meaningful precondition/implementation mismatch in executeSurfacePattern (the interface establishes a caller obligation but the implementation silently absorbs violations) and a @Nullable annotation gap that misleads readers of the signature. These are genuine but isolated issues in an otherwise well-contracted codebase, placing it solidly above the 'significant drag' threshold (60) but short of 'consistently excellent' (85+).

strengths: Guard clauses used consistently across all query executor and exploration methods — null/empty checks placed at the entry point of every public method before any logic runs.
Control flow in the collocate-ranking pipeline (CollocateQueryHelper) is linear and explicit: collect hits, pre-fetch frequencies, score, sort, limit — no hidden branching or unclear short-circuits.
No identical if/else branches or always-true/false conditions observed in any of the 23 reviewed files.
Boolean expressions are direct and unambiguous throughout; every conditional produces or guards a meaningful side effect rather than wrapping a trivial boolean return.
logic_clarity.issue_character: The issues are isolated to a single method (detokenize) and its two call sites in BlackLabSnippetParser: the @Nullable return annotation creates an unreachable null-fallback branch that is dead code in both callers, because both callers always pass a non-null string.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase is largely solid for logic_clarity: guards are correct and consistent, control flow across the 23 reviewed files is predictable, and no structural confusers (identical branches, always-true conditions, async-wrapping-sync) were found. One real finding pulls the score down from the high-80s: the redundant dead-fallback in detokenize callers indicates that the @Nullable contract of detokenize is misleading at its call sites, creating silent dead code in two places. The intentional unreachable patterns (AssertionError after System.exit, unreachable UTF-8 catch) are documented and should not be penalised. Placing this at 80 — solid but with a small, concrete logic clarity gap in one utility class.

strengths: JSpecify @Nullable/@NonNull annotations are applied consistently across the main public API surface: all QueryExecutor parameters, HttpApiUtils method signatures, ExplorationHandlers, MultiSeedExplorer, and ExploreResponseAssembler are fully annotated.

BlackLabSnippetParser uses @Nullable on most nullable-returning methods (extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch return, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, extractPlainTextTokenAt, safeParts, detokenize) — demonstrating clear intent and partial discipline.

GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType is correctly annotated @Nullable on its return type, and RelationPatternUtils.buildFullPattern uses @Nullable for the headword parameter where null is explicitly allowed.
Java generics are used correctly throughout — no raw types or unchecked casts observed in the reviewed files; records and sealed interfaces are used appropriately.
type_safety.issue_character: The issues are annotation omissions in utility classes — methods that defensively handle null at runtime but whose type signatures do not advertise that fact — rather than structural unsafety or missing annotations on the main API surface.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase scores well above average because the public API boundary is carefully annotated and the core query/exploration/HTTP layers use @Nullable/@NonNull consistently. What pulls the score down is a cluster of annotation gaps concentrated in parsing utilities (BlackLabSnippetParser, RelationPatternUtils, Main.getParam, RenderMode.parse) and the test-seam null field in CollocateQueryHelper: these are cases where the declared type says non-null but runtime behaviour is nullable, which is precisely what annotation-based type safety is meant to prevent. The issues are localized (all in helper/utility code, not the API contracts) and mechanical to fix, placing the codebase in the 70–75 range — solid but with an identified cluster of annotation gaps that a type checker would flag.

strengths: Class names are precise domain nouns with no generic verbs: ConcordanceHandlers, CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, ExploreResponseAssembler each communicate their specific role
Model record fields use unambiguous domain vocabulary: avgLogDice, commonalityScore, distinctivenessScore, presentInCount — readable without a glossary
QueryExecutor interface method names cleanly discriminate between five execution modes with distinct names rather than overloads or boolean flags
Config layer follows a consistent Noun/NounLoader/NounSerializer/NounUtils naming pattern across GrammarConfig, RelationConfig, and RelationPatternUtils
Boolean predicate methods on CollocateProfile (isFullyShared, isPartiallyShared, isSpecific) are honest and match the sharingCategory() enum constants they correspond to
naming_quality.issue_character: A small set of abbreviation inconsistencies — 'Dep' vs 'Dependency', 'SENT' vs 'sentence', 'collocMap' vs 'collocate' — appear in three different files, suggesting no single root cause but rather isolated local choices that diverge from the codebase's otherwise consistent full-word convention.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase scores solidly on naming: the large majority of class, method, field, and variable names communicate intent precisely and align with a consistent domain vocabulary. What pulls the score below 85 is a recurring abbreviation inconsistency: the same root concept is abbreviated in some names ('Dep', 'SENT', 'colloc') and spelled out in others ('Dependency', 'sentence', 'collocate') within adjacent code, forcing readers to recognize that these are the same concept. These are localized rather than systemic, placing the score in the 'solid but uneven' band near 80.

strengths: Centralised HTTP error boundary in HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling() is applied uniformly to every registered handler, giving predictable HTTP status codes for IAE, IOException, and unexpected exceptions.
Error context is preserved at every catch-and-rethrow site: all IOException wrappers pass the original exception as cause, maintaining full stack traces.
GrammarConfigLoader's two-tier contract (IOException for file I/O callers, IllegalStateException for startup/DI callers) is explicitly documented and consistently applied.
@Nullable annotations on all null-returning parser utilities (BlackLabSnippetParser, CqlUtils) make the absence-vs-error contract explicit and predictable.
error_consistency.issue_character: The core issue is a single structural misclassification: user-supplied BCQL parse failures (InvalidQuery) are wrapped as IOException at the executor boundary, causing client validation errors to surface as HTTP 500 rather than 400 — and producing dead catch code where the API layer tried to correct for it.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a genuinely well-designed error architecture: a uniform HTTP boundary, consistent cause preservation, and documented multi-tier contracts. A developer navigating error paths would find the top-level and config layers clear and predictable. However, the InvalidQuery→IOException misclassification means that malformed BCQL patterns submitted by clients return 500, and the dead catch in CorpusQueryHandlers shows the inconsistency was noticed but not fully resolved. This places the codebase at the 'solid but uneven' range (80), pulled slightly lower by the concrete downstream impact: callers cannot distinguish server faults from input errors for the BCQL path.

strengths: Fully acyclic runtime dependency graph: no package cycle exists anywhere in the codebase.
ExplorationService interface cleanly interposes a boundary between the api layer and the exploration implementation, keeping ExplorationHandlers free of exploration internals.
model and indexer packages are genuinely isolated — model has zero project imports, indexer imports nothing from the rest of the application.
Package-info files are present for all major packages with succinct, accurate scope descriptions.
Main.java acts as the sole composition root — all wiring is explicit and in one place, avoiding hidden static initialization or import-time side effects.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The issues stem from a single structural asymmetry: the exploration subsystem has a proper service-layer interface (ExplorationService) while the sketch and concordance subsystems lack any equivalent, causing the api layer to absorb query-layer concerns and non-trivial business logic; the secondary issues (RadialPlot misplacement, Javadoc upward reference) are symptoms of the same boundary-thinking gap.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The dependency direction is sound and there are no cycles, which places this codebase well above the 60-range. However, the lack of a service abstraction for the sketch and concordance subsystems means SketchHandlers does double duty as both HTTP handler and domain logic coordinator, creating a high blast radius for any change to sketch query behavior and violating the architectural contract established by the exploration subsystem's cleaner layering. The RadialPlot misplacement and the Javadoc upward reference from config to api are minor but reinforce that boundary discipline is applied unevenly. These gaps collectively prevent a score above 80, landing the codebase around 72: strong skeleton, uneven follow-through.

strengths: System property reads (grammar.config, cors.allow.origin) are deferred to call/request time — not baked into class-load-time field initialisers — making them safe to override in tests.
No environment-variable reads at module scope anywhere in the codebase.
All inter-service dependencies are wired through explicit constructor injection with no hidden global registries or singletons whose creation order could cause boot-order failures.
The shutdown hook in Main uses AtomicReference to correctly separate hook registration from server construction, avoiding the race where a partially-constructed graph leaks resources.
Port binding is explicitly deferred from the WordSketchApiServer constructor to start(), clearly separating object construction from I/O side effects.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The only issues present are mild class-load-time allocations (three separate ObjectMapper instances) and one package-private static field; these are not boot-order or import-time coupling problems but rather a duplication / encapsulation concern with limited blast radius.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This codebase avoids every pattern the initialization_coupling dimension warns against: no import-time I/O, no env reads at field initialisation, no hidden global singletons, no circular lazy imports. The explicit constructor-injection style throughout Main and the API server makes the boot order obvious and auditable. The only blemish is three separate ObjectMapper statics that are harmless today but could diverge if Jackson configuration is ever customised, and one package-private static that leaks the mapper across the api package. These are minor concerns well below the threshold of a scoring penalty, placing the codebase firmly in the 85–90 band — solid with only superficial rough edges.

strengths: Handler class structure is uniform across all five api handlers: same visibility, same constructor shape, same logging idiom, no deviations
Static utility classes follow an identical final-class-with-private-constructor pattern in every package that has them
Enum-with-label pattern (label field + label() accessor + toString() override) applied uniformly to all four domain enums
Every package has a package-info.java, providing consistent discoverability and documentation coverage
Model type strategy (record vs class) is consciously documented in model/package-info.java, even if the rationale does not extend to construction-pattern consistency
convention_outlier.issue_character: The issues are isolated outliers from otherwise strong, codebase-wide patterns rather than systemic drift: a misplaced rendering class, an undocumented asymmetry in construction style between two sibling model classes, and a single stale Javadoc cross-reference.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates exemplary convention discipline in its most-used patterns — handler shape, utility class shape, enum idiom, and package documentation are all applied without a single deviation. What prevents a higher score is a small cluster of localized outliers: RadialPlot belongs to the api package despite being a rendering utility with no HTTP concerns (a style island relative to the rest of api), ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult diverge on construction convention without explanation, and one Javadoc references a class that was never implemented. These issues would momentarily mislead a developer exploring the api package or comparing the two result types, placing the codebase solidly in the 'solid but uneven' band rather than exemplary.

strengths: Shared test infrastructure (StubQueryExecutor, TestExchangeFactory, GrammarConfigHelper) is factored into dedicated classes and reused consistently across all test packages, keeping setup concise
API handler tests cover both happy-path (200) and all major validation failure (400) cases for every handler class
BlackLabIntegrationTest exercises the full collocate pipeline (XML snippet parsing → frequency accumulation → logDice ranking) in a CI-safe way without a live index
No mock-framework coupling; hand-rolled stubs expose only the interface surface and are stable across refactors
No snapshot tests, no timing dependencies, no test-ordering annotations — the suite has no fragility patterns
test_strategy.issue_character: The gap is concentrated at one critical layer: the real BlackLabQueryExecutor (Lucene index queries) is permanently @Disabled in CI, so the actual data-retrieval path is never verified outside of a manually configured environment.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The test suite is well-organised, uses sound patterns, and achieves good coverage of business logic at the exploration, API, and utility layers — this pushes the score above 60. However, the permanently disabled live-index tests mean the most critical production path (Lucene corpus queries) has zero CI coverage, and the live-server test (WordSketchApiServerTest) only exercises the routing layer with an empty stub, providing no cross-module verification of real data flow. By the rubric, 'critical paths with zero test coverage' is a primary indicator; one such path here is the entire data-retrieval layer, which is a subsystem-level gap that keeps the score from reaching solid territory (80+). A score of 68 reflects a strong unit-testing culture with one significant structural hole.

strengths: Handler separation by concern (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) gives the HTTP layer a flat, predictable shape — each handler class is responsible for a single endpoint group.
wrapWithErrorHandling establishes a clear, uniform error contract for the vast majority of endpoints: IAE → 400, IOException → 500, RequestEntityTooLargeException → 413, all returning JSON bodies.
Shared HttpApiUtils.requireParam / parseIntParam / parseDoubleParam helpers enforce a single validation-and-error convention across all query-string parameter extraction.
buildExploreResponseEnvelope centralises the response envelope (status, parameters sub-map, variant fields) across three related exploration endpoints, keeping their response shapes consistent.
ExplorationService, QueryExecutor, and GrammarConfig are injected through constructor parameters in all handlers; no hidden service locator or global state in the public API surface.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The issues are not widespread but are structurally significant at the service-interface boundary: one handler breaks the uniform error contract by catching exceptions inline, and the single-seed exploration API has an asymmetric positional parameter that the codebase's own documentation flagged as a planned refactor (SingleSeedExplorationOptions) that was never completed.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The HTTP API layer is well-structured with consistent handler patterns, shared utilities, and documented error contracts — this pushes the score toward the 70s. Two concrete gaps pull it back: CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery silently bypasses wrapWithErrorHandling for InvalidQuery exceptions, creating a hole in the error contract for the /api/bcql endpoint; and ExplorationService.exploreByPattern's positional int reverseExpansionLimit breaks the symmetry with the multi-seed and comparison methods that bundle all options in ExplorationOptions. The ExplorationOptions Javadoc even references SingleSeedExplorationOptions as the intended fix, making this a known incomplete refactor at the public service boundary. These two issues are localised but affect the reliability and developer-comprehension of the API contract at meaningful boundaries, warranting a score in the upper-60s rather than the low-80s.

strengths: The security model is explicitly and accurately documented in the WordSketchApiServer Javadoc, including a clear warning that any change to the bind address requires an added auth layer.
CORS policy is uniformly applied via a single setCorsHeader() helper called inside every response-sending method — there is no sibling endpoint that escapes the CORS gate.

Input validation helpers (requireParam, parseIntParam, MAX_PARAM_LENGTH, readBodyWithSizeLimit) are used consistently across all five handler classes, so no endpoint accepts user input without length/type checks.
Loopback-only binding (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress()) is enforced in start() with no configuration path to override it from outside the codebase.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The issues are not inconsistency within an existing auth scheme but rather an absence of differentiation between read-only and mutating/privileged endpoints, plus a runtime trust-boundary expansion path via the cors.allow.origin system property that is applied uniformly (consistently) but without per-endpoint safeguards.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase scores above a mid-range baseline because the authorization posture is fully intentional and uniformly applied: every endpoint is unauthenticated, the loopback binding is enforced, and this design is clearly documented. Against the global anchor of 80 (solid but uneven), this falls slightly below because the /api/bcql endpoint — which executes arbitrary corpus queries against the index — receives identical trust treatment as read-only sketch endpoints, and the cors.allow.origin='*' override path has no per-endpoint mitigation. These are not inconsistencies in a multi-tier auth scheme (the skip rules cover that), but they are missed opportunities to add minimal differentiation at the lowest-cost tier (localhost-scoped query caps or CORS origin validation at startup rather than per-request warn-and-continue). The score of 76 reflects a coherent, documented, and consistently applied security model that lacks depth of defence at the points of highest exposure.

strengths: Layer boundaries are sharp and consistently enforced: HTTP concerns, algorithm logic, corpus access, grammar config, and DTOs each live in their own package with no cross-cutting leakage.

Data/computation separation is applied correctly in the config package: RelationConfig is a pure record, RelationPatternUtils owns all pattern-building logic, GrammarConfig owns all config queries — no mixing.
Response-assembly concerns are centralised in ExploreResponseAssembler, keeping handler classes free of serialization logic and making response shape changes single-site.
CollocateQueryHelper and MultiSeedExplorer are well-scoped extractions: each owns exactly one algorithmic concern and is not asked to do more.

ExplorationService's interface boundary is stable and its rationale is documented — the HTTP layer depends on the interface, not the implementation, enabling the implementation split across SemanticFieldExplorer/MultiSeedExplorer/CollocateProfileComparator.
design_coherence.issue_character: Issues are isolated and localized: no systemic layer violation or global structural problem, but a small cluster of incoherences in one handler class (asymmetric response building, awkward two-map parameter) and one configuration class (mixed exception contracts across loading methods), plus one class with a documented but unresolved dual-role tension.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The architecture is fundamentally sound — the layer separation, data/logic split, and extraction of focused helpers all demonstrate deliberate design thinking that a developer would notice and appreciate. The incoherences that exist (SemanticFieldExplorer's dual role, the envelope parameter ambiguity, GrammarConfigLoader's mixed exception contract, handleSemanticFieldExamples's inline response building) are real but all localised to specific classes rather than indicative of a structural pattern problem. This places the codebase above the 60 'significant drag' anchor and close to the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor, docking a few points for the cluster of coherence issues in the exploration layer API surface.

issues.summary: Edge and RelationEdgeType are visualization response primitives housed in model/exploration despite the model package explicitly excluding presentation concerns
issues.suggestion: Move Edge and RelationEdgeType from model/exploration/ into api/ (or a new api/graph/ sub-package if RadialPlot is also moved there). Update ExploreResponseAssembler and any test imports accordingly. evidence: Edge.java is a record with fields source, target, weight, and RelationEdgeType — purely a graph serialization DTO created by ExploreResponseAssembler for API JSON responses.
RelationEdgeType labels ('seed_adj', 'discovered_adj', 'modifier') are stable JSON serialization keys used by the API — serialization keys are a presentation concern, not a domain model concern.
model/package-info.java states explicitly: 'No persistence, I/O, or HTTP concerns belong in this package.' API response DTOs are HTTP concerns.
ExploreResponseAssembler (an api/ class) constructs all Edge instances from domain data — the only producer of Edge objects is the API layer, confirming Edge belongs there.

issues.summary: ExplorationOptions Javadoc references non-existent SingleSeedExplorationOptions, creating a documentation-reality mismatch for the options pattern
issues.suggestion: Either remove the SingleSeedExplorationOptions reference and update the Javadoc to reflect the current design (bare int parameter for reverseExpansionLimit), or implement SingleSeedExplorationOptions extending ExplorationOptions with the additional field and update exploreByPattern's signature to use it. The former is the minimal fix; the latter would improve the API surface.

No class named SingleSeedExplorationOptions exists anywhere in the codebase — grep across src/ finds zero matches.

issues.summary: ExplorationService interface bundles three algorithms but SemanticFieldExplorer owns only one directly, making two of four methods pure pass-through delegations
issues.suggestion: Either collapse the two delegating methods back into SemanticFieldExplorer (if the algorithms are small enough) or split ExplorationService into narrower ports: SingleSeedExplorationService (owned by SemanticFieldExplorer), MultiSeedExplorationService (owned by MultiSeedExplorer), and CollocateComparisonService (owned by CollocateProfileComparator). ExplorationHandlers would then accept the relevant port(s) it actually needs rather than a bundled facade. If the single stable-API-surface rationale is strong, consider adding at minimum a non-trivial coordination step (e.g., shared caching, logging, or metric recording) so the facade layer earns its place.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed (line 434) is: `return multiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection(seeds, relationConfig, opts);` — a one-line delegation with no transformation, policy, or adaptation.
SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles is: `return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seeds, opts);` — again a one-line delegation.

ExplorationHandlers calls all four ExplorationService methods by name, so the unified interface does not simplify the caller — it uses whichever method the HTTP endpoint semantics require, gaining no abstraction benefit from the bundling.

The class Javadoc describes SemanticFieldExplorer as 'a coordination facade' but the two delegated methods perform no coordination — MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator are fully autonomous and could be injected directly into ExplorationHandlers.

issues.summary: ComparisonResult exposes both single-pass summaryCounts() and three redundant re-streaming filter methods at the seam

If they must remain public, consider computing them lazily and caching to avoid repeated traversal. The main goal is to give callers at the seam one obvious, efficient access path (summaryCounts + the collocates list) rather than multiple overlapping ones.
evidence: ComparisonResult exposes fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), and specific() as separate stream passes alongside summaryCounts() (single-pass). issues.summary: executeSurfacePattern documents lemma-in-pattern as a caller precondition but silently returns empty list on violation instead of throwing

issues.summary: Null-fallback after detokenize() is dead code at both call sites
issues.suggestion: Either change detokenize to @NonNull (removing the null-input path and replacing `return text` with `return ""`), or change the two callers to use the result directly without the ternary: `return detokenize(assembled);` and `return detokenize(text);`. The simpler fix is to remove the null-input case from detokenize since all callers guard against null before calling it.
evidence: detokenize(String text) returns null only when its argument is null (line 140: `if (text == null || text.isEmpty()) return text;` — the null return path requires null input).

In trimToSentence() at line 88: `assembled` is produced by string concatenation of non-null prefix/suffix literals and `match`; even if `match` is null, Java string concat yields the literal string 'null', so `assembled` is never null. `detokenize(assembled)` therefore never returns null, and the fallback `? ... : assembled` is never executed.

In extractPlainTextFromXml() at line 130: `text` is the result of `.replaceAll(...).replaceAll(...).trim()` on a non-null `xmlSnippet` (already guarded at line 125). `detokenize(text)` never returns null, so the fallback `? ... : text` is never executed.
The @Nullable annotation on detokenize's return type correctly documents the null-input contract, but since no caller ever passes null, both null-checks are unreachable.

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.index field is set to null in protected test-seam constructor but has no @Nullable annotation
