issues.summary: InvalidQuery (user input error) wrapped as IOException causes BCQL parse failures to return HTTP 500 instead of 400
issues.suggestion: Distinguish client-error parse failures from server I/O failures at the executor boundary. Either (a) wrap InvalidQuery as IllegalArgumentException (which wrapWithErrorHandling already maps to 400) instead of IOException, or (b) introduce a checked QueryParseException that wrapWithErrorHandling explicitly catches and maps to 400. Option (a) is the minimal change: in CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery(), performCollocateSearch(), and BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeCqlQuery(), replace 'throw new IOException("...parse error...", e)' with 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("...parse error...", e)'.
evidence: CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery() line 243: catch (InvalidQuery e) { throw new IOException("BCQL parse error: " + e.getMessage(), e); }
CollocateQueryHelper.performCollocateSearch() line 148: same pattern wraps InvalidQuery as IOException
BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeCqlQuery() line 137: catch (InvalidQuery e) { throw new IOException("CQL parse error: " + e.getMessage(), e); }
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling() maps IOException → HTTP 500, so any InvalidQuery from user-supplied patterns surfaces as a server error to the client

issues.summary: catch (InvalidQuery | ...) in CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery is dead code — executor always wraps InvalidQuery before returning
issues.suggestion: Remove the InvalidQuery branch from the catch in handleCorpusQuery (or remove the entire try/catch if IAE is also handled by wrapWithErrorHandling). The real fix is the companion issue (invalid_query_wrapped_as_ioexception): once InvalidQuery is re-classified as IAE at the executor boundary, wrapWithErrorHandling will correctly return 400 for all paths, and this local catch becomes unnecessary.
evidence: CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery() line 50: catch (InvalidQuery | IllegalArgumentException e) { sendError(exchange, 400, ...) }
executor.executeBcqlQuery() → CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery() always wraps InvalidQuery into IOException before returning; InvalidQuery never propagates past the executor

The IOException from the executor (containing wrapped InvalidQuery) is not caught in handleCorpusQuery, so it reaches wrapWithErrorHandling and produces HTTP 500 — the opposite of what the dead catch intended

issues.summary: CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery catches InvalidQuery inline instead of delegating to wrapWithErrorHandling, breaking the uniform error contract
issues.suggestion: Remove the try/catch from handleCorpusQuery; let BlackLab's InvalidQuery propagate. In wrapWithErrorHandling in HttpApiUtils add `InvalidQuery` (which extends RuntimeException) to the existing catch hierarchy, or re-throw it as an IllegalArgumentException in the executor. This restores the uniform error contract across all endpoints.
evidence: Lines 48-52: `try { ... } catch (InvalidQuery | IllegalArgumentException e) { HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, ...); }` — handler catches and responds directly

All other handlers (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers) throw IllegalArgumentException and let the wrapWithErrorHandling wrapper in WordSketchApiServer produce the JSON error body

The inline catch means unhandled RuntimeException subtypes and unexpected IOException paths in handleCorpusQuery will surface as unformatted JDK HTTP server errors rather than JSON 500 responses, diverging from every other endpoint's behaviour

issues.summary: ExplorationService.exploreByPattern takes a positional int reverseExpansionLimit while multi-seed and comparison methods use only ExplorationOptions, leaving an asymmetric and incomplete public service interface
issues.suggestion: Create the SingleSeedExplorationOptions record (extending or wrapping ExplorationOptions with reverseExpansionLimit) that the Javadoc already describes, and update ExplorationService.exploreByPattern to accept it. This aligns the implemented API with the stated design intent and makes all three service methods accept their options through a typed options object.
evidence: ExplorationService.java line 38: `int reverseExpansionLimit` is a 4th positional parameter to exploreByPattern, absent from exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles signatures

ExplorationOptions.java lines 8-9: Javadoc explicitly notes 'Single-seed exploration uses SingleSeedExplorationOptions, which adds the reverseExpansionLimit parameter' — but no such class exists in the codebase

ExplorationHandlers.java line 67: `semanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(seed, resolvedConfig, opts, nounsPerSeed)` — the HTTP layer must pass this as a raw int, requiring it to know about the single-seed vs multi-seed distinction at call-site

issues.summary: cors.allow.origin system property can be set to '*' at runtime, widening the browser trust boundary across all endpoints with only a logged warning and no startup validation
issues.suggestion: Validate the cors.allow.origin property once in WordSketchApiServer.start() or a static initializer in HttpApiUtils; throw IllegalStateException or log a single WARN with a 5-second startup delay when the value is '*'. This turns a silent per-request log noise into a one-time, hard-to-miss misconfiguration signal.
evidence: setCorsHeader() reads System.getProperty("cors.allow.origin", DEFAULT_CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN) on every call
When the value is "*", it only logs a warning: 'cors.allow.origin is set to "*" — this widens the browser trust boundary across all endpoints'
The warning is emitted on every response, not once at startup, so it appears in logs continuously rather than as a one-time misconfiguration alert
No validation or rejection of the '*' value occurs at server start, so a misconfigured deployment silently widens CORS to all origins for all endpoints including /api/bcql

issues.summary: The /api/bcql endpoint, which executes arbitrary index queries, has the same access controls as read-only sketch endpoints despite being the highest-risk path in the API
issues.suggestion: Add a structured audit log entry for every /api/bcql invocation (at INFO level, logging the pattern and request source) and consider tightening MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH/MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH to values matching actual UI use cases. For any future non-loopback deployment, mark /api/bcql as requiring an explicit opt-in flag (e.g., --enable-bcql-endpoint) to prevent accidental exposure.
evidence: registerPostHandler(server, "/api/bcql", ...) in WordSketchApiServer applies the same wrapWithErrorHandling wrapper as all GET endpoints — no additional gate
parseBcqlRequest validates pattern length (MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH=1024) and bracket depth (MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH=20) but these limits are significantly larger than what any built-in endpoint needs
There is no request logging, audit trail, or IP-source check that would distinguish /api/bcql calls from /health calls in production logs

Sketch endpoints construct queries internally from validated lemma/relation inputs; /api/bcql passes user-supplied pattern strings directly to executor.executeBcqlQuery after only syntactic length checks

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer is both an algorithm owner and a coordination facade, breaking the pattern established by MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator
issues.suggestion: Extract the single-seed algorithm (exploreByPattern and its helpers fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) into a new package-private SingleSeedExplorer class, mirroring MultiSeedExplorer. SemanticFieldExplorer then becomes a thin facade that wires and delegates to all three algorithm classes.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer owns the full single-seed exploration algorithm (exploreByPattern with helpers fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) while simultaneously delegating multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator.

The class Javadoc explicitly labels this 'coordination facade for the HTTP exploration layer' while also describing it as the owner of the single-seed algorithm — two responsibilities named in one sentence.

MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator each have a single focused responsibility; extracting a SingleSeedExplorer (parallel to MultiSeedExplorer) would make SemanticFieldExplorer a pure facade and restore consistency.

issues.summary: buildExploreResponseEnvelope takes two Map<String,Object> parameters whose difference (inner vs top-level placement) is not evident from the signature
issues.suggestion: Replace the two-map parameter pair with a simple record or builder: record ExploreEnvelopeSpec(Map<String,Object> innerParams, Map<String,Object> topLevelFields). Alternatively, move the caller-specific fields into named parameters (e.g. a nullable String seedParam, a nullable List<String> seedsParam) so the method signature encodes the placement intent explicitly.
evidence: Method signature: buildExploreResponseEnvelope(String relationType, CommonExploreParams params, Map<String,Object> paramExtras, Map<String,Object> variantFields) — paramExtras go inside the 'parameters' sub-map; variantFields go at the top level. This placement difference is invisible at the call site.
All three callers pass Map.of() literals inline, making the intent hard to read: e.g. Map.of("nouns_per", nounsPerSeed) vs Map.of("seed", result.seed()) with no structural cue distinguishing them.
The Javadoc explains the difference but a developer reading a call site cannot determine which map goes where without tracing into the method.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExamples builds its response map with 8 inline put() calls while the other three handlers in the same class use buildExploreResponseEnvelope
issues.suggestion: Either document in the method Javadoc why this handler intentionally does not use buildExploreResponseEnvelope (the different response shape), or extract a lightweight buildExamplesResponseEnvelope helper to make the divergence explicit rather than silently inconsistent.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, and handleSemanticFieldComparison all call buildExploreResponseEnvelope to construct the response skeleton.
handleSemanticFieldExamples instead creates a new HashMap and manually calls response.put() 8 times (status, collocate, seed, relation, bcql, top, examples, total_results) with no shared helper.

The examples response structure is genuinely different (no 'parameters' sub-map, different fields), but the asymmetry is not documented and violates the pattern a developer would expect when reading the class.

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader exposes four loading methods with two incompatible exception contracts: two throw checked IOException, two wrap it in RuntimeException
issues.suggestion: Consolidate to one contract per class: keep load(Path) and fromReader(Reader) as the canonical checked-exception API, and move createDefaultEnglish() variants to a separate GrammarConfigFactory or make them static factories on a distinct startup-utilities class. This gives each class a uniform, predictable exception contract.
evidence: load(Path) and fromReader(Reader) both declare throws IOException — callers must handle the checked exception.
createDefaultEnglish(String) and createDefaultEnglish() both catch IOException and rethrow as IllegalStateException — callers get a runtime exception with no checked-exception obligation.

The Javadoc explains this as a 'two-tier exception contract' but a developer discovering the class for the first time must read all four method signatures to understand which contract applies when. The class name gives no hint that two of its methods behave fundamentally differently from the other two.
